coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of magnetic carbon nanodot/graphene oxide hybrid material (Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO) for magnetic solid phase extraction of ibuprofen in human blood samples prior to HPLC-DAD determination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Yuvali, Dönay
Narin, Ibrahim
Soylak, Mustafa
Yilmaz, Erkan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a green production method was used to obtain magnetic carbon nanodot/graphene oxide hybrid material (Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO) for the magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) of ibuprofen (IBU) in human plasma prior to HPLC-DAD determination. For the first time in the literature, Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was synthesized and used as an adsorbent. C-nanodots were produced from pasteurized cow milk by using a simple and cheap hydrothermal method. After production of the C-nanodots and GO, Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was fabricated in green solvent medium by using an one-step hydrothermal method. The method was based on the simple separation, preconcentration and analysis of ibuprofen by using MSPE-HPLC-DAD combination. The concentration changes of ibuprofen in human bloods against time were successfully monitored by using this combined method. For this purpose, blood samples were taken from volunteers at certain intervals after the administration of a certain dose of ibuprofen, and the MSPE method was used to monitor the concentration changes of ibuprofen in the blood samples. Experimental variables affecting the extraction efficiency of IBU such as sample solution pH, amount of adsorbent, extraction time, eluent type and volume were studied and optimized in the details. The characterization studies for the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO were carried out by X-ray diffraction spectrometry (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR), Raman spectrometry (Raman), energy dispersive x-ray (EDX), vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) techniques. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was 8.0 ng mL−1 and the recoveries at three spiked levels in human plasma were ranged from 91.0% to 95.0% with the relative standard deviation (RSD %) less than 4.0 % (n = 6). The results show that together use of MSPE with HPLC-DAD provides a simple and rapid analysis of ibuprofen in human plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal synthesis
Ibuprofen
Magnetic separation
Magnetic hybrid material
Blood
Time dependent analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the amount of adsorbent
Accuracy experiments for the MSPE method
Time-dependent analysis of ibuprofen in blood samples

This work has been supported by Erciyes University Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit with research project TDK-2016-6823. The research project has been proved by Erciyes University Clinical Research Ethics Committee 2016/154 project number.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis steps of the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material (A) and MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure stages (B).

STEM images of graphene (A) and C-nanodots (B), SEM images of C-Nanodots (C), STEM images of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material (D) EDX analysis results of (E) and SEM-Mapping analysis results of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material (F).
XRD spectra of C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials.
Raman spectra of C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials.
FT-IR spectrum of C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials.

Effect of pH on the extraction efficiency of ibuprofen (A) (Experimental conditions: 60 mg of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO, 2 mL of ethanol for desorption, 2 min vortex mixing at 3000 rpm for adsorption and desorption experiments, N = 3), Zeta potential measurement results for Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material.

Effect of elution solvent type on the extraction efficiency of ibuprofen (Experimental conditions: pH of sample solution: 3.0, 60 mg of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO, 2 mL of eluent for desorption, 2 min vortex mixing at 3000 rpm for adsorption and desorption experiments, N = 3).
Application of MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure to human blood sample (n = 5).
Comparison of MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure with other literature studies.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This study is a part of doctorate thesis of Mrs. Donay Yuvali

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of magnetic carbon nanodot/graphene oxide hybrid material (Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO) for magnetic solid phase extraction of ibuprofen in human blood samples prior to HPLC-DAD determination

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ERNAM-Erciyes University Nanotechnology Application and Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a green production method was used to obtain magnetic carbon nanodot/graphene oxide hybrid material (Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO) for the magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) of ibuprofen (IBU) in human plasma prior to HPLC-DAD determination. For the first time in the literature, Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was synthesized and used as an adsorbent. C-nanodots were produced from pasteurized cow milk by using a simple and cheap hydrothermal method. After production of the C-nanodots and GO, Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was fabricated in green solvent medium by using an one-step hydrothermal method. The method was based on the simple separation, preconcentration and analysis of ibuprofen by using MSPE-HPLC-DAD combination. The concentration changes of ibuprofen in human bloods against time were successfully monitored by using this combined method. For this purpose, blood samples were taken from volunteers at certain intervals after the administration of a certain dose of ibuprofen, and the MSPE method was used to monitor the concentration changes of ibuprofen in the blood samples. Experimental variables affecting the extraction efficiency of IBU such as sample solution pH, amount of adsorbent, extraction time, eluent type and volume were studied and optimized in the details. The characterization studies for the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO were carried out by X-ray diffraction spectrometry (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR), Raman spectrometry (Raman), energy dispersive x-ray (EDX), vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) techniques. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was 8.0 ng mL−1 and the recoveries at three spiked levels in human plasma were ranged from 91.0% to 95.0% with the relative standard deviation (RSD %) less than 4.0 % (n = 6). The results show that together use of MSPE with HPLC-DAD provides a simple and rapid analysis of ibuprofen in human plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ibuprofen is (2RS)-1[4-(2-methyl propyl) phenyl] propionic acid that first members of propionic acid derivate and classified as one of the most used non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) since 1970′s [1]. Ibuprofen has been the safest conventional NSAIDs and it hasn’t side effects when used permitted dosages [1–3]. Although ibuprofen is widely used as an analgesic, anti-inflammatory and antipyretic agent, it has very widely indications [2]. Remarkable therapeutic applications of ibuprofen are rheumatoid and osteo-arthritis, patent ductus arteriosus, cystic fibrosis and orthostatic hypotension. Nowadays prophylactic treatment has gained importance all over the world and some studies in the literature have shown that ibuprofen has a significant effect on reducing neurodegeneration and chemopreventive against breast cancer [1–3].

In particular, determination of the concentration of the active substance in human plasma is the most important factor in drug dosage adjustment, patient drug compliance and individual dose adjustment [2]. Frequent and intensive use of ibuprofen has made the analysis of biological samples of great importance due to the wide range of indications and the different effects of varying doses [4–10].

Monitoring how drug active substances change over time in the human body and how their concentration changes plays an important role in pharmacokinetics. To analyze drug active substances in the biological samples such as blood, urine, tissue and so on, separation and preconcentration methods are needed before the analysis of them with sensitive devices such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [11–13]. Because biological samples are very complicated matrix medium, and the concentration of analyte or analytes to be assayed in these matrix mediums may be even below the detection limit that these sensitive devices can determine [4–10].

In recent years, green analytical chemistry (GAC) have gained a very important place in sample preparation methods based on separation and preconcentration of analytes. The basic principles of GAC are the development of new generation techniques in which expensive, hazardous chemical and toxic substances are limited or eliminated, the time and energy consumption and waste generation are minimized, and above all, laboratory safety is kept to a maximum [14].

Increased selectivity with the new generation adsorbents, reusability of adsorbent provide a significant reduction in analysis costs, while the use of less chemicals and less chemical waste contributes more to the development of environmentally friendly methods. The importance of adsorbent in extraction efficiency has led to the need for synthesis of new adsorbents. Many conventional solid phase extraction (SPE) methods have been reported in the literature using different adsorbents such as silica gel, amberlite resins, chelating resin, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide and the like [14–21]. Most of the reported conventional SPE methods are not suitable for the principles of GAC. A new generation of magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) techniques using magnetically separable adsorbents to bring conventional solid phase extraction techniques into line with the principles of GAC has been introduced in the literature [14–21].

Carbon dots (C-dots), a new member of carbon nanomaterials, have been used in the literature for many purposes [22]. C-dots have been fabricated by using different starting material including natural, biological or synthetic sources of carbon. Inert chemical and biocompatible structures, low toxicity, simple functionalization and photoluminescence are important features in the preference of C-dots. These properties of C-points have led to their frequent use in different chemical applications such as biological imaging, optoelectronics, biosensing, drug delivery photovoltaics, and sample preparation [22].

Graphene (G) and graphene oxide (GO) as two-dimensional materials have attracted attention in SPE methods because of their unique chemical resistivity against to harsh experimental conditions, high adsorption capacities, simple functionalization, easy production and low prices. Functionalization of G and GO with other carbon-based nanomaterials and magnetic nanoparticles has been concluded different hybrid materials that present unique properties of the components that make up it [23,24]. Magnetic hybrid nanomaterials have important place in the MSPE of organic, inorganic and bioactive species such as active pharmaceutical ingredients food dyes, major biomolecules, vitamins, heavy metals etc. [23,24].

In this study, green production and usability of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO magnetic nanomaterial was explored as the new generation sorbent for MSPE of ibuprofen in blood samples prior to HPLC-DAD determination. The unique advantages of GO, C-nanodots and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles were combined in this study. The Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO-based MSPE method was successfully used to monitor changes in concentration of ibuprofen in human blood samples over time. Blood samples were taken from volunteer men and women.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the C-nanodots, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials were conducted by an advanced X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8). A Raman spectrophotometer (WITec alpha 300 M + micro-Raman system with 532 nm laser source) was used for the Raman analysis of the C-nanoots, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials. The FT-IR characterizations of the GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO were conducted by Perkin-Elmer 400 FT-IR spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). STEM, SEM, EDX and SEM-Mapping analysis were carried out on the FE-SEM (ZEISS Gemini 550) and SEM (LEO 440) instruments. The surface charge or surface zeta potentials of the as-synthesized hybrid material were determinated at different pH medium via a Malvern ZEN2600 Zetasizer Nano ZS. The magnetic properties of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO was measured at room temperature with VSM. An Agilent 1200 series high performance liquid chromatograph equipped with a diode array detector for chromatographic analysis. A pH meter (Inolab, WTW pH720, Germany), a vortex (Velp Scientifica ZX3, England) was used to improve the extraction efficiency. Neodymium magnet was used to collection and the separation of magnetic Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO material.

Analytical purity chemicals and ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm Millipore Elix 5 (Millipore, USA) were used throughout the experiments. Ibuprofen (purity >99.5%) was obtained from İbrahim Ethem-Menarini drug company (Turkey). Stock solution of ibuprofen (100 μg mL−1) was prepared in methanol and stored at 4 °C before use. The HPLC grade acetonitrile was purchased from Merck (Germany). Graphite powder obtained from Sigma Co. (Steinheim, Germany) was used for the production of GO. Pasteurized cow’s milk obtained from Kayseri market in Turkey was used in production of C-nanodots. 100 mM pH 3.0 phosphate buffer solution was prepared by mixing 100 mM % 85 (w/w) ortho phosphoric acid and 100 mM di-sodium hydrogen phosphate salt.

GO was fabricated from graphite powder by using the modified Hummer method [23]. Pasteurized cow’s milk obtained from Kayseri markets in Turkey was used in production of C-nanodots. For this purpose, 20 mL of deionized pure water was added to 25 mL of cow’s milk and the resulting mixture was transferred to hydrothermal synthesis unit. The synthesis medium was a metal unit called a hydrothermal synthesis reactor in which a Teflon vessel was placed according to its dimensions. The mixture was allowed to react at 180 0C for 12 h. The C-nanodots obtained at the end of the reaction were separated from the liquid phase by centrifugation (4000 rpm for 10 min.), washed twice with purified water, once with ethanol and dried in an oven at 75 0C.

In the synthesis of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material which used as adsorbent in the MSPE, hydrothermal synthesis method as an easy and cheap nanomaterial production method was used. For this purpose, 1.0 g of FeCl3 and 4.0 g of sodium acetate was dissolved in 50 mL of ethylene glycol and then 0.5 g of GO and 0.25 g of the previously produced C-nanodots were added to the resulting solution. The resulting mixture was subjected to ultrasonic vibration for 15 min to distribute the GO and C-nanodots homogeneously to the whole solution. The resulting mixture was taken into the hydrothermal synthesis reactor and allowed to react for 8 h. in an oven set at 180 ℃. At the end of this period, a magnet was used to separate the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO particles obtained from the liquid phase. The water phase separated from the solid phase was discarded. Then, distilled water was added to the solid phase twice and washing procedure was performed. Subsequently, washing was carried out once with acetone and the obtained magnetic Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was dried in the oven at 75 0C. Synthesis steps of the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material are shown in Fig. 1 A.

15 mL of aliquot sample solution containing known concentration amount of ibuprofen and pH 3.0 buffer solution were placed in a screw-cap glass centrifuge tube. Then 60 mg of fabricated magnetic Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was added into the same tube. The resulting mixture was vortexed at 3000 rpm for 2 min. After analyte molecules were adsorbed on the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO particles, the particles were simply isolated by a neodymium magnet on side of tube. Aqueous phase was removed by a simple pipette and then, 2.0 mL of ethanol was added. The mixture was vortexed for 2 min. to elute the adsorbed ibuprofen molecules. The eluent phase was injected to HPLC vials after they filtrated by a 0.45 μm filter and HPLC analysis were carried out. The same procedure was applied for the blank samples. Calibration curve was created by applying the MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure to the standard solutions at optimal conditions. The MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure is schematized in Fig. 1B.

For chromatographic analysis of ibuprofen, we applied the similar method which reported by Bazregar et al. (2016) [25]. A C18 chromatography column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm, HiChroma, UK) was used for the separations. The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile- phosphate buffer solution (pH 3.0, 50 mM), (67:33, v/v). The sample injection volume was 20 μL and determination wavelength was 220 nm.

Permission for the collection of human blood samples was obtained from the Erciyes University Clinical Research Ethics Committee with 2016/154 project number. Blood samples were taken from volunteer patients and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Clear blood samples were stored at −20 °C prior to MSPE extraction. Before the MSPE application, 1.0 ml acetonitrile was added to 0.5 mL of blood sample to remove plasma proteins. The developed procedure was then applied to supernatant under the optimum conditions.

Although Graphene (G) and graphene oxide (GO) can be classified in the most commonly used new generation materials, an important problem that limits the practical use of G and GO materials in addition to their significant use advantages in adsorption based studies is the restacking and aggregation of the layers as a result of increasing of interaction forces between carbon layers have π- π bonds. The restacking of carbon layers results in aggregation, minimization of high surface area and blocking of active adsorption sites of G and GO. Practical use problems of G and GO resulting from agglomeration of the carbon layers can be solved by dispersing the nanoscale particles between these carbon layers and creating a space between these layers. Functionalities of G and GO with other carbon based nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds and nanodots) and metal/metal oxides (Ag, Pd, Au, Cu, TiO2, ZnO, SiO2 etc.) are is one of the main way to solve aggregation and restacking problems of G and GO solutions. In order to solve restacking and aggregation problems of the GO, we modified GO with C-nanodots by dispersing C-nanodots into carbon layers of GO. Our material was then modified with Fe3O4 nanoparticles in order to isolate the C-nanodot@GO hybrid material from the solution medium with magnetic field effect after the adsorption and desorption of the analyte,

The C-nanodots, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid materials were characterized by using STEM, FE-SEM, SEM-Mapping and EDX, Raman, XRD and FT-IR techniques. STEM images of graphene and C-nanodots, SEM images of C-Nanodots and STEM images of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material are shown in Fig. 2 A-D. According to STEM analysis, GO has a transparent image. This transparent image shows that GO with minium layer number has been successfully synthesized from graphite (Fig. 2A). STEM and SEM analysis given in Fig. 2B and Fig. 2C show that the particle size of the produced C-nanodot particles is in the range of 100−150 nm on average. When STEM images of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material are examined, it is seen that C-nanodots and Fe3O4 particles in spherical structure are formed in different regions of GO sheets homogeneously without the damage of the layered structures during the synthesis (Fig. 2D). Moreover, Fe3O4 formation was confirmed by SEM-EDX and SEM-Mapping analyzes (Fig. 2E and Fig. 2F).

The XRD spectra of the C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material is shown in Fig. 3 A, Fig. 3B and Fig. 3C, respectively. 2θ: 19.7, 21.4, 26.5, 28.4, 31.4 and 34.8 angles belong to C-nanodot appear to be consistent with the literature [26]. When the XRD spectrum of GO is examined, it is seen that the peaks at 2θ: 11.7 and 42.3 angles are consistent with the literature [27]. When XRD spectra of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was examined, it was observed in literature studies that peaks at 2θ: 28.4, 36.8 and 57.2 angles belong to Fe3O4 particles [28]. Compared with GO, as the peak intensity of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material for 2θ = 11.66° is obviously reduced, and the peak at 2θ = 42.34° totally disappears. It indicates that Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO was exfoliated largely, and more disordered stacking and less agglomerated GO sheets consist in the nanomaterial.

Raman spectra of C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO materials are shown in Fig. 4 . It has been proved by literature studies that peaks at 1576 and 1346 cm−1 belong to G and D bans of C-nanodot [29]. It is easily understood from the fact that the D band of GO is more dominant than the G band in which graphene oxide is successfully synthesized from graphite. However, when the Raman spectrum of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO materials obtained as a result of modification is examined, it is observed that the D band strength is increased due to the modification of C-nanodot with GO, the D band where G band is suppressed and the D band peak intensity is G band peak intensity. The severity ratio is significantly changed.

FT-IR spectrum of C-nanodot, GO and Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO materials are shown in Fig. 5 . For GO, the specific absorption peaks are obtained at around 3240 cm−1, 1700 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 belong to the OH, CO and COC stretching vibrations, respectively. The results showed that fabrication of GO by starting graphite powder was succesful. CC stretching vibration peaks around 2900 cm-1 confirmed the formation of C-Nanodots. When FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material is examined, it is observed that O-H vibration peak is still dominant, peak intensities of CC vibration peaks around 2900 cm−1 for C-Nanodots is reduced, peaks around 1700 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1 belong to CO and CC vibrations for GO are still dominant and a specific peak for the Fe-O stretching vibrations is observed at around 520 cm−1. The results for Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material confirmed that Fe3O4 nanoparticles and C-nanodots were successfully formed on the different region of GO. Moreover when compared with the FT-IR spectrums of C-nanodots and GO, it is reached that there are important shifts in the peak regions and important changes in the peak shapes and peak intensities in Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material (Fig. 5).

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO was examined by using a vibrating sample magnetometry at an room temperature. Maximum saturation of the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO was found to be 24.2 emu g−1 (Supplemental Fig. S1).

Due to adsorption of analytes in aqueous sample solution on to adsorbent is the basis of solid phase extraction applications, pH is one of the most important parameters. pH creates the most suitable medium for both analyte and adsorbent to achieve best interactions. Hence, the effect of the pH of sample solution on the adsorption efficiency of ibuprofen were studied in the range of 2.0–10.0 by applying the MSPE method to buffered model solutions containing 0.2 μg·mL−1 ibuprofen. The results obtained are given in Fig. 6 A. The adsorption mechanism of ibuprofen on the adsorbent is illuminated by measuring the zeta potential of the surface charge of the adsorbent at different pH values (Fig. 6B). The results show that the synthesized adsorbent surface is positively charged up to pH 4.5, above this pH the adsorbent surface is negatively charged. The pKa of ibuprofen is 4.6 as an acidic drug. It is mean that while ibuprofen it keeps its neutral form lower than pKa value, above pH 4.6 ibuprofen gain negative charge and gain more negative charge with higher pH values. While pH of sample solution is changed from 4.6 to higher pH, negative form of ibuprofen is formed. Therefore, thrust forces start between negatively charged ibuprofen surface and negatively charged adsorbent surface above the pKa of ibuprofen and adsorption efficiency decreases. So give rise to increase on the adsorption at pH values lower than pH 4.6. Zeta potential measurement results are compatible with effect of pH results. Thus, the adsorption mechanism for pH < pKa can be explained by other mechanisms such as hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic attraction and π–π interactions. For pH < pKa (ibuprofen), ibuprofen and GO protect their COOH functional groups as neutral since both do not have ionization. Therefore, COOH functional group of GO can form strong hydrogen bonding with COOH groups of ibuprofen. The π-π interaction is another possible mechanism of interaction between Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO and ibuprofen since ibuprofen and GO have π-π bonds. This investigation suggested that non-electrostatic adsorption mechanisms such as hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction could contribute to the adsorption of ibuprofen between pH 2.0 and 4.0. According to our analysis results, maximum peak area and minimum standard deviation were obtained at pH 3.0. Hence optimum pH values for the aqueous sample phase was selected as pH 3.0 for the further experiments.

Desorption of the analytes from the adsorbent surface is a critical issue where the eluent type and volume have to be optimized parameter to reach maximum elution efficiency and recoveries for the analyte or analytes. In our procedure, the desorption of ibuprofen from the surface of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO was examined by using various solvents. The results based on the peak area showed that the maximum elution efficiency for ibuprofen can be achieved by using ethanol as eluent (Fig. 7 ). Hence ethanol as eluent was used for next stages of our procedure. To reach the optimum eluent volume, the experiments were conducted at different volumes of ethanol between 0.5 mL and 2.0 mL. It can be seen from Supplemental Fig. S2. that 2.0 mL of ethanol was sufficient to desorp ibuprofen adsorbed on the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO particles. Therefore, further experiments were continued by 2.0 mL of ethanol as eluent. The effect of vortex time for desorption on the recovery of ibuprofen was investigated for sufficient interaction of the adsorbent and eluent solution in the elution process. The vortex duration was screened in the range of 2−20 min and it was determined that the quantitative elution of ibuprofen was performed rapidly by vortexing for 2 min (Supplemental Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of adsorbent
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of adsorbents used in solid phase extraction methods is of vital importance for both successful adsorption process and re-desorbing of analytes after adsorption. Because, as the amount of adsorbent should not be lower than the required amount in order to extract all of the analyte of interest in the sample solution medium to the adsorbent, using more adsorbents than required may prevent the adsorbed analyte from re-desorbing. In our study, the effect of the adsorbent amount was checked by employing Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material doses changed from 20 mg to 100 mg. The results illustrated in Supplemental Fig. S4. showed that the highest peak area and the lowest standard deviation for ibuprofen was obtained with addition of 60 mg of Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO. Therefore, further experiments were carried out by using 60 mg of the adsorbent.

The stability of adsorbents against to the experimental environment and, the reusability of the adsorbents without losing their performance for many times is important to develop economical and more green methods. Reusability and stability tests of the synthesized Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material were checked on the model solutions by using the same experimental conditions for each time. The results showed that as the MSPE performance of our material did not changed for the repeated apply up to twelve cycles of our MSPE method, more than twelve cycles, extraction performance of our material decreases (Supplemental Fig. S5). The obtained results proved that new synthesized Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO could be used repeatedly for at least twelve times.

Under the optimum conditions, analytical performance parameters including the limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), linear range, correlation coefficient, intra-day precision and accuracy, and inter-day precision and accuracy values for the MSPE–HPLC procedure were studied and the obtained values are shown in the Table 1 . Linear regression analyses were carried out by using the peak areas against the concentrations of the ibuprofen after application of the developed MSPE method. The LOD and LOQ were calculated according by ICH guideline that validation of analytical procedures. As calculation of LOD was based on the dividing three times the standard deviation of the response to the slope of calibration curve, LOQ was based on the dividing ten times the standard deviation of the response to the slope of calibration curve. The intra-day and inter-day precision and accuracy values of the Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO based MSPE method were calculated by applying our method to six independent samples including known concentration of ibuprofen during the same day (intra-day) and different days (inter-day), respectively. Lower limit of quantification was defined. The accuracy should be ± 2 of nominal concentration from ≥ five replicates and the precision should be ± 20% coefficient of variation (CV) from ≥ five replicates at LLOQ concentration. Calibration standards were prepared in plasma at the following concentrations: 15.0 (Lower limit of quantification, LLOQ), 50, 100, 250, 500, 750, 1000 and 2000 ng·mL-−1. Qualtiy Concentrations (QCs) was decided according to FDA bioanalytical guidelines [30]. Low, medium and high QCs were prepared at concentrations of three times of LLOQ, %30 of calibration curve and %75 of calibration curve, respectively. Intra-run accuracy and precision was determined from the mean values from six replicates of each QC. Inter-run batch accuracy and precision were determined using the mean values from each QC concentration six runs for plasma sample.

Accuracy was regarded as acceptable if within ±15% of the nominal concentration for each QC and within ±20% at the LLOQ. Precision was regarded as acceptable if the coefficient of variation (CV) was ≤ 15% for each QC and ≤20% at the LLOQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accuracy experiments for the MSPE method
ce:section.ce:para: To test the accuracy of the proposed MSPE method in the analysis of real samples, the optimized MSPE method was used to a blood sample taken from adult male aged 22 years. For this purpose, known concentrations of ibuprofen were added to the blood sample taken from the volunteer and the blood sample was prepared to the MSPE application by following the sample preparation method described in section 2.5. After application of MSPE, concentrations of ibuprofen in eluent phase were measured by HPLC-DAD and recovery results were calculated by using added and recovered ibuprofen concentrations (Table 2 ). The chromatograms belong to the blank human blood sample and spiked human blood sample are given in Supplemental Figure S6. Retention time of ibuprofen is 4.005 min. After application of the MSPE method, it is seen that a predominant peak belongs to foreign molecule in the retention time of 1.241 min on the chromatogram of the blank blood sample don’t appear after MSPE method. It is understood that our method shows selectivity for ibuprofen in the plasma matrix. Acceptable recoveries changed from 91% to 95% were obtained for the blood sample by applying the our MSPE method. The obtained results shown in Table 2 proved that our method could be apply to blood samples for the accurate and sensitive analysis of ibuprofen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-dependent analysis of ibuprofen in blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: After completing the accuracy experiments, the developed MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure was applied to the time-dependent analysis of ibuprofen in blood samples. For this purpose, blood samples were taken from volunteers (Patient A was a male aged 23 years and Patient B was a female aged 22 years) at certain intervals after the administration of a certain dose (400 mg) of ibuprofen and the developed MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure was used to monitor the concentration changes of ibuprofen in the bloods. Time-dependent concentration changes of ibuprofen for male and female volunteers are presented in Supplemental Fig. S7.

Ibuprofen absorption is rapid and complete when administered orally. Ibuprofen is available as an over-the-counter medication throughout the world with the usual recommended adult daily dosage of 200–400 mg every 6 h. Ibuprofen has a wide therapeutic window, between 10–50 mg L−1, and a toxic concentration of >100 mg L−1 [2,3]. Our analysis results are compatible in literature and clinical research. There is evidence of a relationship between therapeutic and/or toxicological effects and blood concentrations of the drug. Future research should try and delineate the therapeutic/toxicological relevance of the pharmacokinetic data and the clinical importance of unresctricted concentrations. Many patients may not respond to a particular NSAID and toxicological profiles may differ between NSAIDs. adjustment dose and therapeutic drug monitoring according to the patient is important.

In this study, Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was synthesized by using green starting material and green synthesis approaches. The as-prepared Fe3O4@C-nanodot@GO hybrid material was later adopted for the efficient magnetic solid phase extraction of ibuprofen from the blood samples with satisfactory results including qualified recoveries, low LODs, LOQs and high precision. The developed MSPE technique proved to be an effective strategy for rapid separation and preconcentration of ibuprofen in complex matrices such as human blood. Furthermore, the time-dependent concentration change of ibuprofen in human blood was successfully monitored by using this MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure. A comparison of the proposed MSPE-HPLC-DAD procedure with some studies in literature is presented in Table 3 . As can be seen, limit of detection and repeatability of our method are comparable or better than existed methods. Moreover, our method allows the time-dependent analysis of ibuprofen in blood samples. The most important feature of our adsorbent is to achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium in as short as 2 min. This fast equilibrium makes our method faster than others. Therefore, it can be a practice application for sensitive and fast time-dependent determination of ibuprofen in human plasma.

All persons who meet authorship criteria have been listed as authors, and all authors certify that they have participated sufficiently in the work to take public responsibility for the content, including participation in the concept, design, analysis, writing, or revision of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by Erciyes University Scientific Research Projects Coordination Unit with research project TDK-2016-6823. The research project has been proved by Erciyes University Clinical Research Ethics Committee 2016/154 project number.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2019.113001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
